alt="Imam Christopher AbdulKareem Pavlicek leads prayer at Ojala Islamic Center in Berwyn, Sunday, March 1, 2026. Ojala is the first Latino mosque in the Midwest. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f8bfd95/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7a%2Fd5%2F12daa7f24ed69c8043095b34c67f%2Flatino-muslims-260304-014.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/28ed67d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7a%2Fd5%2F12daa7f24ed69c8043095b34c67f%2Flatino-muslims-260304-014.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f8bfd95/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7a%2Fd5%2F12daa7f24ed69c8043095b34c67f%2Flatino-muslims-260304-014.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Imam Christopher AbdulKareem Pavlicek leads prayer at Ojala Islamic Center in Berwyn.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>In 2018, the nonprofit Ojala Foundation was formed as a way to foster connections between the Latino and Muslim communities. Pavlicek is a co-founder of the foundation. </p><p>Foundation members have spent time serving to the needs of those less fortunate, including providing food to the homeless in Chicago’s Latino neighborhoods, including Little Village, Pilsen, Humboldt Park and Marquette Park.</p><p>After eight years of raising funds, the Ojala Foundation took possession of the church structure on May 5 of last year. Soon after, it began removing all the symbols and wooden benches you’d typically find in a Christian church. </p><p>Just a few weeks ago, workers completed installing carpet in the main prayer space.</p><p>“I've been in this work for over 20-plus years. When we started doing all of this work, it was because there was a very sincere need from our side, as new converts to the faith, not being able to find anyone that was like us,” said Alma Campos, who sits on the Ojala Foundation board and was a co-founder. </p><p>Campos converted to Islam nearly three decades ago after being brought up Catholic. Nearly 80% of Mexicans are Roman Catholic, according to a 2020 <a class="Link" href="https://www.state.gov/reports/2022-report-on-international-religious-freedom/mexico" target="_blank" ><u>survey</u></a> by the Mexican government.</p><p>Her best friend introduced the religion to her.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-420000" name="image-420000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/19fbb06/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7868x4416+0+416/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F84%2F00%2Fd1c957224648ae724f4136e99a80%2Flatino-muslims-260304-012.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f23990f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7868x4416+0+416/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F84%2F00%2Fd1c957224648ae724f4136e99a80%2Flatino-muslims-260304-012.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f28e791/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7868x4416+0+416/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F84%2F00%2Fd1c957224648ae724f4136e99a80%2Flatino-muslims-260304-012.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Men perform prayer at Ojala Islamic Center in Berwyn, Sunday, March 1, 2026. Ojala is the first Latino mosque in the Midwest." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f28e791/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7868x4416+0+416/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F84%2F00%2Fd1c957224648ae724f4136e99a80%2Flatino-muslims-260304-012.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5334d91/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7868x4416+0+416/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F84%2F00%2Fd1c957224648ae724f4136e99a80%2Flatino-muslims-260304-012.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f28e791/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7868x4416+0+416/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F84%2F00%2Fd1c957224648ae724f4136e99a80%2Flatino-muslims-260304-012.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Men perform prayer at Ojala Islamic Center in Berwyn on Sunday. Ojala is the first Latino mosque in the Midwest.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“It’s been an ongoing effort for a very long time," she said. "Seeing it culminate in this, and us being able to purchase our own space where we could have all of the things that we were not able to have and provide that for the community — it's an amazing feeling."</p><p>Ojala member Christopher Nevarez-Azdar’s family is from Mexico and also grew up in a Roman Catholic household.</p><p>Nevarez-Azdar converted in 2018, the first in his family, as an undergraduate student at University of Illinois Chicago.</p><p>He said part of the mission of the mosque is to reach out to the Latino community and to help those who wish to convert to Islam. But non-Latinos are welcomed as well, he said.</p><p>“The idea of Ojala is to be a space where people who become Muslim feel comfortable. Most of us happen to be Latino, but a lot of them are not Latino," said Nevarez-Azdar, 28, an assistant public defender in Cook County. </p><p>"We also have folks that are part of our community, that aren't Muslim but may be interested in Islam. You have people from our community. We have South Asian representation, Arab representation, West African, North African."</p><p>Nevarez-Azdar sees a lot of similarities between Islam and the Catholic faiths.</p><p>“You have a lot of family values that are very universal to both religions. The importance of women and respecting women and respecting one's mother. It's truly very important," he said.</p><p>Ojala Islamic Center, he said, allows Latino Muslims to practice their faith in a language they understand by offering sermons, lectures and classes in Spanish. </p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-930000" name="image-930000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7a22982/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F22%2F99%2F061863374596826f53d6c2df0fe4%2Flatino-muslims-260304-001.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/aa6bd07/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F22%2F99%2F061863374596826f53d6c2df0fe4%2Flatino-muslims-260304-001.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ce87fad/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F22%2F99%2F061863374596826f53d6c2df0fe4%2Flatino-muslims-260304-001.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Ven Howard-Paez (center) prepares food for iftar at Ojala Islamic Center in Berwyn, Sunday, March 1, 2026. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ce87fad/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F22%2F99%2F061863374596826f53d6c2df0fe4%2Flatino-muslims-260304-001.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/906b872/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F22%2F99%2F061863374596826f53d6c2df0fe4%2Flatino-muslims-260304-001.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ce87fad/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F22%2F99%2F061863374596826f53d6c2df0fe4%2Flatino-muslims-260304-001.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Ven Howard-Paez prepares food for iftar at Ojala Islamic Center in Berwyn on Sunday.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“The way that we present Islam to people, it's a universal message. But it's presented in a way that we would understand it. That's why we think that there was that need for a Latino mosque, especially in a super Latino area," he said.</p><p>For Ojala’s 200 or so members, the opening of the mosque comes at a perilous time.</p><p>Since last fall, U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement have targeted Latinos nationwide and in Chicago in President Trump’s aggressive deportation initiative.</p><p>And, over the weekend, Trump partnered with Israel in ordering an attack on Iran.</p><p>Those who are Muslim and Latino could feel they’re being targeted on two fronts.</p><p>Pavlicek said he’s heard hurtful comments from people targeting Ojala’s members for being both Latino and Muslim.</p><p>“There were people making comments like, 'Double deport. Deport them for both reasons,'” he said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-710000" name="image-710000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8cfc1f5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8089x4540+0+428/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe2%2Fa0%2Ffab8028c4fc99345382d459afb8b%2Flatino-muslims-260304-009.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6dcc93b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8089x4540+0+428/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe2%2Fa0%2Ffab8028c4fc99345382d459afb8b%2Flatino-muslims-260304-009.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/311e5cb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8089x4540+0+428/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe2%2Fa0%2Ffab8028c4fc99345382d459afb8b%2Flatino-muslims-260304-009.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Board Member Alma Campos (far right) prays at Ojala Islamic Center in Berwyn, Sunday, March 1, 2026. Ojala is the first Latino mosque in the Midwest. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/311e5cb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8089x4540+0+428/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe2%2Fa0%2Ffab8028c4fc99345382d459afb8b%2Flatino-muslims-260304-009.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c36096c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8089x4540+0+428/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe2%2Fa0%2Ffab8028c4fc99345382d459afb8b%2Flatino-muslims-260304-009.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/311e5cb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8089x4540+0+428/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe2%2Fa0%2Ffab8028c4fc99345382d459afb8b%2Flatino-muslims-260304-009.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Board Member Alma Campos (far right) prays at Ojala Islamic Center in Berwyn on Sunday.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>It impacted fundraising for the new center. Some events had to be done virtually for the safety of Ojala’s members in September, he said.</p><p>"We have a lot of people in our community that are undocumented, and other people that are here completely legal. But they're getting picked up too," Pavlicek said. "People were really hesitant to come out and come be a part of things. It greatly affected us."</p><p>Alma Campos said it’s a challenging time, but that's no different than any other time.</p><p>“Being a Latina that is first generation born and raised here, I never thought that I would see the day that I would hear the stories of earlier generations when ICE was really ‘la migra.' I never thought that I would see the day where I would be able to see that for myself," she said. "It's something that definitely weighs heavy on us because, at the end of the day, these are our brothers and sisters in humanity. So we are here for our Latino people. We are from the Latino people.”</p><p><i>Michael Puente is a reporter and weekend anchor for WBEZ. Reach him at </i><a class="Link" href="mailto:mpuente@wbez.org" target="_blank" ><i><u>mpuente@wbez.org</u></i></a><i>. </i></p>
entries.author: Michael Puente

entries.title: Questions cloud Mormon church handling of suburban congregation's ex-leader accused of abusing minors
entries.summary: <p>A member of a prominent Mormon family who was arrested in Utah in recent months on child exploitation charges previously lived in the Chicago area and for many years attended a congregation in the far northwest suburbs — where he's accused of abusing other minors.</p><p>Questions are now being raised about whether local — or even national — Mormon leaders knew of that abuse but kept it secret, failing to tell police and providing cover that allowed child abuse to perpetuate.</p><p>Wade Christofferson, who is in his early 70s, was arrested in November, charged with sexual exploitation crimes against minors and accused in court records of molesting an Ohio child 15 to 20 times.</p><p>A brother of an influential Mormon leader, Christofferson “also allegedly engaged in a sexually explicit FaceTime call with a second minor victim and sent coded letters to her Utah home that referenced sexual activity,” federal prosecutors allege. “Christofferson also committed hands-on sexual abuse of that child as well.”</p><p>While that court case focuses on more recent accusations of sexual abuse out of state, Christofferson is accused of molesting other children years earlier while a member and leader of a Mormon — or Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints — congregation in Crystal Lake.</p><p>Initially, he was "excommunicated" by the church for that behavior, according to interviews. But he was later allowed to return as a full member — and leader.</p><p>A one-time member of Christofferson's McHenry County congregation, former Woodstock resident Edward Nachel, told the Chicago Sun-Times that he was part of the church tribunal that convened in the mid-1990s over allegations that Christofferson molested at least one child at a “slumber party” hosted by one of his children.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-2e0000" name="image-2e0000"></a> <picture 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/053ce10/2147483647/strip/true/crop/990x556+0+147/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4f%2F21%2F87403e1e4ce2b7de2653d1ce9b69%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-01-at-10-55-58-pm.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Edward Nachel.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Facebook, Mormon Stories podcast</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>While church leaders subsequently “excommunicated” Christofferson over the alleged misconduct, they apparently never went to police or told their flock, and Christofferson was allowed to continue being a part of the congregation, though with a downgraded status.</p><p>“No authorities were brought in, nothing was ever mentioned” to the membership, Nachel said, adding that a rumor was allowed to persist that Christofferson's discipline had to do with an extramarital affair.</p><p>“Nothing was ever done to dissuade this guy from doing this stuff.”</p><p>At some point, Christofferson was “rebaptized,” and he moved to Ohio and resumed leadership roles, apparently without rank-and-file church members being told of his past.</p><p>It's unclear who signed off on Christofferson's return.</p><p>Nachel said Christofferson’s recent arrest in Utah spurred him to speak up. He said he took his oath seriously at the time of the tribunal that the proceedings and outcome were supposed to be secret, but he also says that had he or others spoken up long ago, perhaps they could have “stopped 30 years” of alleged abuse since then.</p><p>Nachel, who moved from the Chicago region to Utah in 2018, said he harbors guilt over what happened and has gone on Facebook to apologize to victims and families for not doing more sooner.</p><p>Current leaders of the Crystal Lake Mormon church couldn’t be reached. Crystal Lake police records show a female accuser came forward in 2022 alleging abuse at the Crystal Lake church by Christofferson years earlier when she was a child.</p><p>Criminal charges weren’t pursued because the alleged assault occurred long enough ago that the statute of limitations had expired, records show.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-770000" name="image-770000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9861206/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1554x872+0+169/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff6%2F1e%2F0213a0014e92bee353cf33d5e1ae%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-01-at-10-07-56-pm.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a44ca75/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1554x872+0+169/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff6%2F1e%2F0213a0014e92bee353cf33d5e1ae%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-01-at-10-07-56-pm.png 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/20e958e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1554x872+0+169/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff6%2F1e%2F0213a0014e92bee353cf33d5e1ae%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-01-at-10-07-56-pm.png" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A Crystal Lake police report showing a child sex abuse allegation against Wade Christofferson while he was involved with the Mormon church in the far northwest suburb. Criminal charges weren't lodged because the statute of limitations had expired by the time police learned of the alleged misconduct, records show." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/20e958e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1554x872+0+169/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff6%2F1e%2F0213a0014e92bee353cf33d5e1ae%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-01-at-10-07-56-pm.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7f7e569/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1554x872+0+169/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff6%2F1e%2F0213a0014e92bee353cf33d5e1ae%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-01-at-10-07-56-pm.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/20e958e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1554x872+0+169/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff6%2F1e%2F0213a0014e92bee353cf33d5e1ae%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-01-at-10-07-56-pm.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A Crystal Lake police report showing a child sex abuse allegation against Wade Christofferson while he was involved with the Mormon church in the far northwest suburb. Criminal charges weren’t lodged because the statute of limitations had expired by the time police learned of the alleged misconduct, records show.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Crystal Lake police department</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The allegations relating to Christofferson’s November arrest indicate he wrote letters to one of the child victims with messages labeled as “top secret” with coded words for body parts, telling the Utah victim: “It was nice to see Snow and her friends when we FaceTimed.”</p><p>Christofferson also allegedly wrote: “I can’t wait to see you and play * games! I have a new one to teach you called ‘school.’ You will like it!”</p><p>His phone’s online history revealed searches for “criminal defense attorneys sex crimes columbus ohio” and “In Ohio do clergy have to report child abuse confessions,” according to federal authorities.</p><p>Floodlit, an organization that chronicles sexual abuse within Mormonism, found after speaking to “multiple survivors or relatives of survivors” that Christofferson “has at least seven to 10 alleged victims from the Crystal Lake” church jurisdiction “in the 1980s and 1990s.”</p><p>Neither Christofferson nor his attorney could be reached for comment.</p><p>Floodlit also reported that D. Todd Christofferson — Wade Christofferson’s brother who is a high-ranking Mormon official, known as an apostle — knew in or about 2018 of at least one abuse allegation, citing information from an accuser.</p><p>A Latter-day Saints spokesman in Utah released a statement to the Sun-Times saying:</p><p>"The Church strongly condemns and does not tolerate abuse and honors the courage and respects the rights of survivors. Regarding the history of Wade Christofferson: President  D. Todd Christofferson was never in a position to know about and, in fact, did not know about or influence the ecclesiastical decisions regarding his brother’s membership."</p><p>"While he and other family members were told of Wade’s... excommunication, he was not informed of the specific reasons and had no reason to suspect it was for abuse."</p><p>"It was not until around 2020 that President Christofferson first learned of — through family disclosure — some of his brother's history of abuse from thirty years earlier; he respected the adult victims' wishes not to involve law enforcement at that time."</p><p>"However, upon learning of a recent allegation involving a minor, President Christofferson immediately reported it to legal authorities."</p><p>"Wade Christofferson was readmitted to the Church in 1997, following established disciplinary and confession processes, and we are aware of no abuse involving his Church service after that time. We remain committed to protecting the vulnerable and ensuring our reporting processes prioritize victim safety above all else."</p><p>Federal authorities haven’t said whether more charges are possible, and how many accusers from the Chicago region they’re aware of.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-f70000" name="image-f70000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d9276ad/2147483647/strip/true/crop/630x354+0+202/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F98%2F53%2F472894154fb4b1fcaaf5e888671a%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-01-at-10-14-09-pm.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/96e1d63/2147483647/strip/true/crop/630x354+0+202/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F98%2F53%2F472894154fb4b1fcaaf5e888671a%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-01-at-10-14-09-pm.png 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d6fd319/2147483647/strip/true/crop/630x354+0+202/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F98%2F53%2F472894154fb4b1fcaaf5e888671a%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-01-at-10-14-09-pm.png" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Mormon church leader D. Todd Christofferson." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d6fd319/2147483647/strip/true/crop/630x354+0+202/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F98%2F53%2F472894154fb4b1fcaaf5e888671a%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-01-at-10-14-09-pm.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b3a830a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/630x354+0+202/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F98%2F53%2F472894154fb4b1fcaaf5e888671a%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-01-at-10-14-09-pm.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d6fd319/2147483647/strip/true/crop/630x354+0+202/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F98%2F53%2F472894154fb4b1fcaaf5e888671a%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-01-at-10-14-09-pm.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Mormon church leader D. Todd Christofferson.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Like <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/11/10/chapelstreet-church-geneva-evangelical-sexual-abuse-baptist-denomination" target="_blank" >other Christian denominations</a> including the Catholic church and the Southern Baptists, the Mormon church has been mired in sex abuse and financial scandals in recent years, though those troubles haven’t gotten much attention in Illinois because the faith group isn’t very large here compared to some Western states.</p><p>In 2023, the Securities and Exchange Commission accused the church and its investment manager of hiding assets to “obscure the amount of the Church’s portfolio” that, by 2018, had reached about $32 billion.</p><p>The church has been hit with numerous claims about minors suffering sex abuse and ecclesiastical officials covering up — with the Guardian newspaper reporting in 2025 that more than 100 allegations against Mormon leaders emerged in California amid a three-year window that allowed adults to get around the expired statute of limitations and file lawsuits if they were molested as children.</p><p>California has more than 700,000 Mormons.</p><p>John Dehlin, host of the Mormon Stories podcast whose web site says he was excommunicated by the church in 2015 as "a direct result of his expanding podcast and outspoken support for the LGBTQ+ community," hosted Nachel last month on his show and observed the irony of critics being kicked out of the church while men like Wade Christofferson are allowed to return and flourish.</p><p>"They excommunicate the critics that are trying to protect the vulnerable and the marginalized," Dehlin said. "It's just suffocatingly painful, the disparity."</p><p>The church's general handbook says: "Some people who are repenting have broken civil or criminal laws. In some cases, government authorities are not aware of this. Bishops and stake presidents encourage members to follow the law and report such matters when required. Leaders also counsel members to obtain competent legal advice when reporting. The Church’s policy is to obey the law."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-2c0001" name="image-2c0001"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/634a26f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/800x449+0+26/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F01%2F25%2Fb2fc68984eb48617f2f6abac8c96%2Fnauvoo-temple-lds.jpeg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1a4897d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/800x449+0+26/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F01%2F25%2Fb2fc68984eb48617f2f6abac8c96%2Fnauvoo-temple-lds.jpeg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1070555/2147483647/strip/true/crop/800x449+0+26/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F01%2F25%2Fb2fc68984eb48617f2f6abac8c96%2Fnauvoo-temple-lds.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A rebuilt Mormon temple in downstate Nauvoo." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1070555/2147483647/strip/true/crop/800x449+0+26/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F01%2F25%2Fb2fc68984eb48617f2f6abac8c96%2Fnauvoo-temple-lds.jpeg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c0fda22/2147483647/strip/true/crop/800x449+0+26/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F01%2F25%2Fb2fc68984eb48617f2f6abac8c96%2Fnauvoo-temple-lds.jpeg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1070555/2147483647/strip/true/crop/800x449+0+26/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F01%2F25%2Fb2fc68984eb48617f2f6abac8c96%2Fnauvoo-temple-lds.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A rebuilt Mormon temple in downstate Nauvoo.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>"In many places, priesthood leaders are required by law to report some illegal behaviors of which they become aware. For example, some states and countries require that child abuse be reported to law enforcement authorities."</p><p>In Utah, there are 2.2 million Mormons, about the size of the Catholic church in Cook and Lake counties.</p><p>There are 17.5 million Mormons worldwide.</p><p>The church reports roughly 60,000 members and nearly 120 congregations in Illinois, including suburban worship sites in Elgin, Glenview and Wilmette.</p><p>Illinois holds an early and ugly place in Latter-day Saints history.</p><p>The church’s web site says: “Thousands of early members . . . fled from Missouri to western Illinois in 1839 to escape persecution, including a literal extermination order given by Missouri governor Lilburn W. Boggs.”</p><p>“The Latter-day Saints drained swamplands on the eastern banks of the Mississippi and established the thriving community of Nauvoo. The city, whose name means ‘beautiful place,’ grew to rival Chicago in size and became a hub of Church activity and commerce in Illinois.”</p><p>“Unfortunately, the peace which Latter-day Saints had initially enjoyed in Illinois lessened with time as the number of Church members grew. Church founder Joseph Smith was martyred by a mob in the neighboring city of Carthage in 1844. By 1846, the Latter-day Saints were compelled to abandon Nauvoo for their own safety, thus launching one of the largest forced migrations in American history and the birth of the Mormon Trail.”</p><p>Smith’s legacy has been challenged in more recent times because he not only had multiple wives — 30 or more — some were already married to his friends. At least one was 14 years old.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2021/2/5/22266185/blase-cupich-catholic-religious-orders-priests-abuse-archiocese-chicago-augustinians-richard-mcgrath" target="_blank" >Cardinal Blase Cupich is keeping secrets regarding abusive order priests, other clerics</a> </li> </ul> </div> <div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/09/30/north-side-rabbi-jewish-school-yeshiva-ohr-boruch-veitzener-cheder-west-rogers-park-dovid-akiva-shenkman" target="_blank" >Rabbi overseeing Jewish elementary school asked judge to go easy on man guilty of child porn</a> </li> </ul> </div> <div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2024/1/26/24041886/child-sex-offender-muslim-scholar-mohammad-saleem-institute-of-islamic-education-elgin" target="_blank" >Muslim scholar who’s a convicted child sex offender helps oversee Islamic boarding school in Elgin</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Robert Herguth

entries.title: As music director for the Oscars, Chicago native Michael Bearden brings elegance, spontaneity
entries.summary: <p>When <a class="Link" href="https://michaelbearden.com/" target="_blank" >Michael Bearden</a> served as music director for the 2025 Academy Awards, he received personal texts and phone calls with a common message.</p><p>“Wow, we’ve never heard this on the Oscars!”</p><p>They were responding to the frenzied violins of Barry White’s “Love’s Theme,” the funky melody of Quincy Jones’ “Ai No Corrida” and the Latin rhythms of Stevie Wonder’s “Another Star.”</p><p>By strategically placing earworms in a night of orchestral music, Bearden appeals to a broad range of viewers while conveying what he calls the “majesty” and “elegance” of the occasion. Those efforts garnered an Emmy nomination for the veteran keyboardist and composer, who has worked with everyone from Michael Jackson and Madonna to Lady Gaga and D’Angelo.</p><p>The Chicago native will take the reins for the second time at the 98th Oscars on March 15 at the Dolby Theatre at Ovation Hollywood. The show will air live at 6 p.m. Central on ABC and Hulu. This year, he will be responsible for conducting and co-arranging nearly 60 pieces of music, including live performances. Likening the job to completing a jigsaw puzzle, Bearden said he promises to bring the same level of precision, while leaving room for spontaneity. But his ultimate goal is to elicit emotion.</p><p>“We’re focused on human connection and togetherness,” said Bearden, who now lives in Los Angeles.<b> </b>“If you’re going to tune in for three hours, I want you to feel something. So the music will reflect that, but it will also reflect what’s happening on the show.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-660000" name="image-660000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="97th Oscars, Academy Awards, Telecast" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/22aff47/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4b%2F35%2Fe7dda7514581bdcc0845d1f76a4a%2F97-tc-2686.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3cb2fef/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4b%2F35%2Fe7dda7514581bdcc0845d1f76a4a%2F97-tc-2686.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/22aff47/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4b%2F35%2Fe7dda7514581bdcc0845d1f76a4a%2F97-tc-2686.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Chicago native Michael Bearden (center, in white) received an Emmy nomination for music direction for the 97th Academy Awards in 2025. He will resume the role at the 98th Academy Awards on March 15.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Trae Patton / The Academy</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Music from the most-nominated film, “Sinners,” and the breakout animated musical “KPop Demon Hunters” will be performed live, according to <a class="Link" href="https://www.hollywoodreporter.com/movies/movie-news/2026-oscars-best-song-performances-golden-sinners-i-lied-1236490437/" target="_blank" ><u>early reports</u></a>. Both movies are nominated for Best Original Song. In addition to overseeing those performances, Bearden and his lead arranger choose the music accompanying presenters and the “bumpers,” or brief selections that are played before commercials. They must also decide how to showcase the scores of several films in bite-sized pieces. On top of all of that, Bearden composes original music for some segments.</p><p>“It’s like a movie director who’s in charge of every department," Bearden said. "I’m in charge of every musical department. I’m like the coach of a team." </p><p>While some awards shows, like the Golden Globes, are now experimenting with DJs instead of orchestras, Bearden said live musicians not only convey grandeur but also bring a level of flexibility that can’t be replaced. That trait came in handy last year when Mick Jagger presented an award during the show.</p><p>“I played one of the Rolling Stones’ famous songs, ‘Sympathy for the Devil,’” Bearden said.</p><p>“When Mick came out and heard that, he just lit up and almost did a show. He went into his Mick Jagger-ness and I just kept the orchestra playing. And then he went back to his presenter role and I stopped the orchestra. You have to be in the moment.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="200" height="113" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/3CjcziQXmsg?feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen title="Mick Jagger Electrifies The Oscars With A Hot Bob Dylan Take"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p></p><h2>Learning from Quincy Jones</h2><p>As a child, Bearden was inspired when he saw Quincy Jones become the first Black musical director and conductor for the Oscars in 1971. Gazing upon Jones in his black tux and ruffled, power-blue shirt, Bearden realized that he, too, could accomplish such a feat.</p><p>“He’s almost like the blueprint of what I do,” Bearden said. “He passed away right before I did it, so he wasn’t able to see me do it.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-240000" name="image-240000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/aecdb09/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1151x646+0+98/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F84%2F6b%2F637164f4410db9880bb5394061c5%2Fquincy-mb-1.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b7853ef/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1151x646+0+98/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F84%2F6b%2F637164f4410db9880bb5394061c5%2Fquincy-mb-1.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a11c209/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1151x646+0+98/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F84%2F6b%2F637164f4410db9880bb5394061c5%2Fquincy-mb-1.jpg" alt="Quincy__MB 1.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a11c209/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1151x646+0+98/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F84%2F6b%2F637164f4410db9880bb5394061c5%2Fquincy-mb-1.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d5cce7b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1151x646+0+98/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F84%2F6b%2F637164f4410db9880bb5394061c5%2Fquincy-mb-1.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a11c209/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1151x646+0+98/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F84%2F6b%2F637164f4410db9880bb5394061c5%2Fquincy-mb-1.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Michael Bearden (right) said he was inspired to pursue a music career when he saw his future mentor, Quincy Jones, become the first Black person to serve as music director and conductor for the Oscars in 1971.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of Michael Bearden</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>But Bearden was able to thank Jones for his influence, and he was even mentored by the icon during his career. Still, there have only been a few other Black people to oversee the music for the Academy Awards in its nearly 100-year history.</p><p>“Hopefully, some young woman or young man will see me do it and go, 'I want to do that, too,'" Bearden said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="200" height="150" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/zm4NalNc-vw?start=96&feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen title="The Opening of the Academy Awards in 1971"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p>Like Jones, Bearden was born on the South Side. His musical journey, which he will chronicle in a forthcoming book, began at an early age when he took piano lessons. Before he graduated from Whitney M. Young Magnet High School, he was already being mentored by Chicago jazz legend Ramsey Lewis. </p><p>After studying at Howard University, Bearden got his big break touring with jazz flutist Herbie Mann. Since then, he has built a storied career, working with more than 500 artists, serving as co-music director for the Emmy Awards and music director for Lady Gaga’s Super Bowl halftime show in 2017. He is perhaps best known as the music director for “This Is It,” Michael Jackson's planned London residency that was canceled after his death and documented in a posthumous 2009 documentary.</p><div halftime show</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-8f0000" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Chicago native Michael Bearden (third from right) poses at a press conference for the 97th Academy Awards, where he served as music director. He will resume the role at the 98th Academy Awards on March 15.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Al Seib / The Academy</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>No matter the work, Bearden said he wants to leave an emotional impact. </p><p>“That’s what I’m trying to do every time I do any sort of awards show or stage show,” he said. “I want you to be moved.”</p>

entries.author: Mike Davis | WBEZ

entries.title: South Side dance company returns to honor members killed in crash: 'We gonna keep it going for both of them'
entries.summary: <p>For just the second time in as many weeks, kids streamed into a basement room at Hamilton Park field house in Englewood.</p><p>The Empiire Dance Company is a renowned group, best known locally for its repeated appearances at the city’s annual Bud Billiken parade. They generally practice twice a week, sometimes double that if a big event is on the horizon.</p><p>But Wednesday night, they rehearsed for a performance at group member Isaiah “Bo” Flemister’s “final curtain call” after he and another core member of the dance company died in a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/02/19/2-killed-3-injured-in-rollover-crash-on-bishop-ford-on-far-south-side" target="_blank" ><u>crash on the Bishop Ford</u></a> last month.</p><p>This weekend, the group plans to dance in sync with a video of Flemister's footwork routine, filmed two days before the crash.</p><p>“It’ll be like he’s dancing with us,” said Latisha Waters, co-CEO of Empiire Dance Company.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-e00000" name="image-e00000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/382bf41/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Faa%2F7d%2F5308cc03471b84a1238637780800%2Fdancerskilled-2603xx-6.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Latisha Waters co-CEO of Empiire Dance Company speaks to a Sun-Times reporter in the basement of the Hamilton Park field house, Wednesday, March 4, 2026. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>A vehicle carrying five members of the dance group flipped in the inbound lanes near 111th Street early in the morning of Feb. 19, authorities say. Flemister, 17, and Lazarus “Hollywood” Gonzalez, 25, were both killed. Three others in the vehicle were injured. One of them is “still fighting for her life in the hospital,” Waters said.</p><p>The group hadn’t practiced since the crash — until Monday.</p><p>Waters said she was late for that practice after struggling to get out of bed. Wednesday was better, she said, after younger members of the group — which has dancers aged 4 to 37 — brought a positive energy. Coming together was what they needed, and what Flemister and Gonzales would have wanted, she said.</p><p>The group is a family, Waters said. The 26-year-old dance group has taught the children of former students.</p><p>“We do everything from Thanksgiving dinners, Christmases, graduations, proms, birthdays, parent-teacher conferences,” Waters said. “We’re tight knit, we’re always together. … We needed to come back in here and be around each other.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7607092/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd3%2F32%2F73b498874c97887e3df58c902f4b%2Fdancerskilled-2603xx-36.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A group of Dancers with the Empiire Dance Company take a break during practice for an upcoming ensemble in the basement of the Hamilton Park field house, Wednesday, March 4, 2026. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Flemister and Gonzales made a name for themselves in their eight years in the group. Members have felt their absence.</p><p>Flemister was a “sponge” with a knack for quickly picking up choreography, according to Dwayne Moore, the other co-CEO of Empiire Dance Company. Moore said he stunned competitors with his quick footwork.</p><p>Gonzales was a talented rapper who wrote about his queer identity, despite being discriminated for it, Moore said.</p><p>Lanayna "GNYY" Williams, an elite dancer with Empiire Dance Company, said the group intends to keep doing majorette in Gonzalez' unique “Hollywood way.” Williams said she admired his dedication. When Gonzalez was shot several years ago, he was kept from dancing for only a few weeks, she said.</p><p>Flemister memorial is set for noon Saturday at the Harold Washington Cultural Center, 4701 S. King Dr.</p><p>Services for Gonzalez are tentatively scheduled for March 20. One of the group’s biggest competitions of the year will happen one day later.</p><p>“It’s definitely going to be hard,” said Moore’s younger brother Shamar "Tek Shala,” a floor instructor with the group. “But it helps seeing the smiles on these kids' faces.”</p><p>“They definitely gonna live through me,” Williams, 22, said. “We gonna keep it going for both of them.”</p><div class="Enhancement" entries.author: Violet Miller

entries.title: Rev. Jesse Jackson to be laid to rest at Oak Woods Cemetery, 'part of the city's unrivaled historic fabric'
entries.summary: <p>Greenwood Avenue, just south of 67th Street, marks the entrance to one of the most exclusive neighborhoods in the city, in a manner of speaking, with its wooded, rolling landscapes, curved roads and a quartet of small lakes.</p><p>You'll find a who's who of mayors, civil rights leaders, athletes, scientists and business people, along with Chicagoans from nearly every walk of life.</p><p>The locale is the 173-year-old Oak Woods Cemetery. And this week, its solemn, historic and picturesque grounds will become the final resting place of the Rev. Jesse Jackson.</p><p>"Graceland Cemetery [on the North Side] is very well-known and casts a very long shadow and is one of those places where if you're looking at historic cemeteries in the U.S. is sort of in that top 10," Charles Birnbaum, president and CEO of the Washington, D.C.-based Cultural Landscape Foundation, said. "But Oak Woods is yet another great cemetery of substantial scale that is part of the city's unrivaled historic fabric."</p><p>Roughly bounded by 67th Street, Cottage Grove Avenue, 71st Street and the Metra Electric tracks, Oak Woods was chartered by the state in 1853 and took its first burials in 1865.</p><p>Chicago was in its infancy when Oak Woods was planned. There were about 30,000 residents then, and the historic events that would shape the city, such as the Great Chicago Fire of 1871 and the 1893 World's Columbian Exposition, were years away.</p><p>But Chicago was a growing city, and the Oak Woods Cemetery Association wanted to create burial grounds that befitted a metropolis on the rise.</p><p>The group hired landscape gardener Adolph Strauch to create the cemetery. The Prussian-born Strauch helped revolutionize 19th-century cemetery design, much like Frederick Law Olmsted and Calvert Vaux improved parks architecture during the same time.</p><p>Strauch was a leader in "landscape lawn" design, a movement of the day that sought to make expansive cemeteries that were planned, laid out and planted like parks and recreational spaces — a substantial step up from simple church burial yards and backyard plots.</p><p>His magnum opus is the stunning 730-acre Spring Grove, a cemetery and arboretum in Cincinnati with hills, 12 small lakes, natural springs and a wealth of architecturally splendid tombs, mausoleums and grave markers.</p><p>But Strauch put in some very fine work in Chicago as well, giving Oak Woods hills, curving streets with names such as Sunset Boulevard and Memorial Drive.</p><p>The monuments there represent nearly two centuries of funerary design, inspired by Egyptian, Greek and Roman revival, Art Deco, modern and contemporary rendered in granite and marble.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-a30000" name="image-a30000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fc4f442/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd3%2Fff%2F2565f5a1400d8fde9510da03627d%2Foakwoods-260228-11.jpg width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5cbc8b0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd3%2Fff%2F2565f5a1400d8fde9510da03627d%2Foakwoods-260228-11.jpg" alt="Rows of grave markers at Oak Woods Cemetery" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5cbc8b0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd3%2Fff%2F2565f5a1400d8fde9510da03627d%2Foakwoods-260228-11.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6437f20/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd3%2Fff%2F2565f5a1400d8fde9510da03627d%2Foakwoods-260228-11.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5cbc8b0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd3%2Fff%2F2565f5a1400d8fde9510da03627d%2Foakwoods-260228-11.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Rows of grave markers at Oak Woods Cemetery</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Oak Woods has a naturalist feel and is visually striking under clear skies, during the verdant summer months. Fall colors are particularly magnificent.</p><p>"What you have are the plantings, the monuments, the ground plane — which is a manicured lawn — forming a unified composition," Birnbaum said. His organization advocates landscapes across North America and has studied <a class="Link" href="https://www.tclf.org/oak-woods-cemetery" target="_blank" >Oak Woods</a>.</p><p>"There's no fences around family burial plots. The eye is able to, just like the visitor, explore the grounds freely without those types of interruptions."</p><h3>Beautiful and once restricted</h3><p>The list of notables interred at Oak Woods is as distinguished as the cemetery's design — perhaps more so.</p><p>Jackson's name will join the roll of a silent city of 200,000, many of whom like he have made local, national and international impact.</p><p>A small stone monument marks the graves of journalist, educator and civil rights leader Ida B. Wells-Barnett, who died in 1931, and her husband, attorney Ferdinand Barnett, who passed away five years later.</p><p>Olympian Jesse Owens, who died in 1980, rests by an Oak Woods lake. His marble monument is engraved with Olympic rings.</p><p>The seemingly endless list of famous people buried at Oak Woods includes Roebuck "Pops" Staples of the Staples Singers, scientist Enrico Fermi, former Chicago Mayor Harold Washington and his successor Gene Sawyer, John H. Johnson and his wife Eunice, both of Johnson Publications fame. Even Kenesaw Mountain Landis, professional baseball's first commissioner, is there.</p><p>Oak Woods also has the largest Confederate burial ground in the North, where 4,000 rebel soldiers who died at Chicago's Civil War-era prison Camp Douglas rest.</p><p>And while the cemetery is probably best-known for the Black people buried there, Oak Woods had turned away Black burials and cremations until protests in 1963 by the NAACP, Rev. Clay Evans and Rev. A.R. Leak of A.R. Leak Funeral Home stopped the racist practice.</p><p>"They organized a march from our funeral home to Oak Woods — thousands of people," Leak's grandson, Spencer Leak Jr., vice president of Leak and Sons Funeral Home, said. "That's what started Oak Woods to open up. I think it took about two weeks."</p><p>Leak and Sons planned and executed Jackson's complex funeral arrangements. </p><p>That his burial at a beautiful place that was changed for the better by protest is perhaps a fitting valedictory for the civil rights giant.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-5c0000" name="module-5c0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><ul id="rte-2f5a1752-1354-11f1-9468-4ffaee5f793a"><li><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/02/18/friends-and-family-say-chicago-embraced-and-nurtured-civil-rights-icon-jesse-jackson-from-the-start" >Friends and family say Chicago embraced and nurtured civil rights icon Jesse Jackson from the start</a></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2026/02/17/rev-jesse-jackson-chicago-mayors-a-fraught-and-contentious-relationship" >Rev. Jesse Jackson and Chicago mayors — a tense, fraught relationship</a></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2026/02/21/how-jesse-jackson-shaped-chicago-education-from-protesting-school-segregation-to-reopening-dyett-hs" >How Jesse Jackson shaped Chicago education, from protesting school segregation to reopening Dyett HS</a></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/in-the-loop-with-sasha-ann-simons/2026/02/19/how-the-late-rev-jesse-jackson-was-a-pioneer-of-the-progressive-movement-and-lgbtq-ally" >Listen: How the late Rev. Jesse Jackson was a pioneer of the progressive movement and LGBTQ+ ally</a></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/money/2026/02/20/jesse-jackson-economic-employment-justice-boycott-civil-rights" >For Jesse Jackson, economic justice and civil rights were intertwined</a></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/columnists/2026/02/18/lights-camera-jesse-jackson-civil-rights-leader-pop-culture-presence" >Lights, camera, Jackson: Civil Rights leader also became a pop culture presence</a></li></ul></div> </div><p></p>

entries.title: South Siders dare to dance after loss of loved ones
entries.summary: <h3>Good morning, Chicago. ✶</h3><p>🔎 <b>Below</b>: After the deaths of two of their members, a tight-knit South Side dance company grapples with performing again — and honoring their loved ones' legacies.</p><p>🗞️ <b>Plus</b>: A growing network of gardens, gas prices spike and more news you need to know.</p><p>📧 <b>Subscribe</b>: <a class="Link" href="https://newsletter-hub.suntimes.com/" target="_blank" >Get this newsletter delivered to your inbox weekday mornings.</a></p><p>⏱️: <i>An 8-minute read</i></p><hr><p></p><h4>TODAY’S WEATHER class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjhkill-l-t/" target="_blank" >South Side dance company returns to honor members killed in crash</a></h3><h3></h3><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjhkill-l-i/" target="_blank" >Violet Miller</a></h5><p><b>Lost loved ones</b>: On Wednesday night, The Empiire Dance Company rehearsed for what they say is a final curtain call for late members Isaiah "Bo" Flemister and Lazarus "Hollywood" Gonzalez. Flemister, 17, and Gonzalez, 25, died Feb. 19 in a <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjhkill-l-d/" target="_blank" >crash on the Bishop Ford</a>, when a vehicle carrying five members of the dance group flipped. The three others were injured, and one is "still fighting for her life in the hospital," said Latisha Waters, co-CEO of Empiire.</p><p><b>The performance</b>: Monday and Wednesday marked the first times the group had rehearsed since the accident. At a memorial for Flemister to be held Saturday at the Harold Washington Cultural Center in Bronzeville, dancers plan to perform in sync with a video of Flemister’s footwork routine, filmed two days before the crash. "It’ll be like he’s dancing with us," Waters said. </p><p><b>'We're tight-knit'</b>: Since its inception 26 years ago, The Empiire Dance Company — which has members ages 4 to 37  — has become like a family, even teaching the children of former students. "We do everything from Thanksgiving dinners, Christmases, graduations, proms, birthdays, parent-teacher conferences,” Waters said. "We’re tight-knit, we’re always together … We needed to come back in here and be around each other."</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjhkill-l-h/" target="_blank" target="_blank" >Growers provide healthy food to people with little access, aim to narrow 20-year life expectancy gap</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjhkill-l-u/" target="_blank" >Kristen Schorsch</a></h5><p><b>Growing network</b>: Sammie Taylor and his wife, Angie, have managed their Fulton Street Flower and Vegetable Garden for about 20 years. It’s part of the Garfield Park Garden Network, one of several programs run by the nonprofit Garfield Park Community Council. Fruits and vegetables grown at 12 community gardens, managed by residents or schools, are sold every other week from June through October at the Council’s Neighborhood Market.</p><p><b>The hope</b>: The garden network aims to make healthy food more accessible in a community where people are expected to die earlier than in any other neighborhood in Chicago, and where residents have fewer nutritious food options. The Taylors hope their garden will help narrow the so-called death gap. "I’m a firm believer that if we eat better, we do better and we can live better," Taylor told WBEZ.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: Chronic conditions including heart disease as well as cancer are leading drivers of the stark<a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjhkill-l-o/" target="_blank" > 20-year life expectancy gap</a> across the city. The death gap is the widest between West Garfield Park, where most residents are Black and can expect to live until 67, and the Loop, where the majority are white and life expectancy is 87 years old. </p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjhkill-l-b/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>CHICAGO HISTORY ⏳</h4><div class="Enhancement" Jesse Jackson will be laid to rest at historic Oak Woods Cemetery</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjhkill-l-p/" target="_blank" >Lee Bey</a></h5><p><b>Historic place</b>: The 173-year-old Oak Woods Cemetery, with its solemn and picturesque grounds, will become the final resting place of the Rev. Jesse Jackson. Near 67th Street, Cottage Grove Avenue, 71st Street and the Metra Electric tracks, Oak Woods was chartered by the state in 1853 and took its first burials in 1865. The list of notables interred at Oak Woods is as distinguished as the cemetery’s design — perhaps more so.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: Jackson’s name will join the roll of a silent city of 200,000, many of whom have also made local, national and international impacts: Ida B. Wells-Barnett, Jesse Owens, Roebuck "Pops" Staples, former Mayor Harold Washington. Oak Woods also has the largest Confederate burial ground in the North, where 4,000 rebel soldiers who died at Chicago’s Civil War-era prisoners of war camp rest. Oak Woods had turned away Black burials and cremations until protests in 1963 by the NAACP, Rev. Clay Evans and Rev. A.R. Leak of A.R. Leak Funeral Home stopped the racist practice. <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjhkill-l-x/" target="_blank" >Leak and Sons</a> have planned Jackson’s complex funeral arrangements.</p><p><b>Jackson's services</b>: A "People’s Celebration" honoring Jackson begins <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjhkill-l-m/" target="_blank" >10 a.m. Friday at Pullman church House of Hope</a>. Former Democratic Presidents <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjhkill-l-c/" target="_blank" >Joe Biden, Barack Obama and Bill Clinton are expected to attend</a> the Friday service. Private homegoing services are scheduled for 10 a.m. Saturday at Rainbow PUSH.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjhkill-l-q/" target="_blank" ><b>READ La Shawn Ford, whom the congressman endorsed while announcing his retirement last summer.</p><p><b>Key election links</b>: <br></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; margin-top: 20px; margin-bottom: 20px; margin-left: 24px; padding: 0px; list-style-type: disc;" id="rte-3b4a0750-189f-11f1-a90d-6bbec16766c1"><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjhkill-l-jr/" target="_blank" >Voter Guide</a></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjhkill-l-jy/" target="_blank" >Build your ballot tool</a></li></ul><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjhkill-l-jj/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p> </p><h4>LET’S HEAR FROM YOU 🗣️</h4><p><b>What political race on the Illinois primary ballot do you care the most about and why?</b></p><p><a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" ><i>Reply to this newsletter</i></a><i> (please include your first and last name). class="Figure-credit"><p>Giacomo Cain/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjhkill-l-tr/" target="_blank" >David Byrne readies his immersive new Chicago theater show</a></h3><p></p><h5>Reporting by <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjhkill-l-ty/" target="_blank" >Mike Davis</a></h5><p>David Byrne stood in a room with an absurd amount of doors, most of which didn't actually lead anywhere.</p><p>"We’re taking the route the audience would take," Byrne explained. “I'm not spoiling too much, but every door looks the same, and they can hear sounds coming from some of these doors. Laughter, conversation."</p><p>Thus began WBEZ reporter Mike Davis' February tour of "Theater of the Mind," Byrne's immersive theater installation in an office building on the edge of the Loop that will premiere March 11.</p><p>The show will allow 16 people at a time to walk through a series of rooms led by a tour guide named David, who will be clad in a seersucker shirt, shorts, and sandals with socks. The outfit is identical to one worn by a 2-year-old Byrne in a photo displayed on a wall.</p><p>"Theater of the Mind” is produced in partnership with the Goodman Theatre as part of the historic company’s 100th season. The exhibition is set to fill 15,000 square feet of ground floor space in the Reid Murdoch Building Downtown.</p><p>Read about our tour of the new production with the Grammy-, Oscar-, Tony- and Golden Globe-winning rock veteran <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/theater/2026/02/05/david-byrne-chicago-theater-of-the-mind-goodman-talking-heads" target="_blank" >here</a>.<br><a </div></div><hr><p></p><h4>YOUR DAILY QUESTION ☕️</h4><p>Yesterday, we asked you: <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjhkill-l-td/" target="_blank" >What’s the best Chicago-themed gift you’ve ever received — or given?</a></p><p>Here’s some of what you said…</p><p>"The best Chicago-themed gift came from my Mom, years ago. It was a set of 4 glasses, each with a famous Chicago site or skyline etched into the glass, including Marshall Field's iconic store. I love them." — <i>Christine Bock</i></p><p>"A photo book of Pope John Paul II’s visit to Chicago, showing him driving past our house on his way to Five Holy Martyrs church in 1979." — <i>Terry Sullivan</i></p><p>"I took a couple of Gino's pizzas enclosed in dry ice in my suitcase to Arizona to meet a friend I'd made online who had never been to Chicago. I also took some Fanny May chocolates. Everything was a big hit, except for the pizza aroma on my clothes that had been in the suitcase." — <i>Janet Ma'ly</i></p><hr><p>Thanks for reading the Sun-Times Morning Edition!<br>Got a story you think we missed? <a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >Email us here.</a></p><hr><p><b>Written and curated by</b>: Matt Moore<br><b>Editor</b>: Eydie Cubarrubia</p><hr><p><i>The Chicago Sun-Times is a nonprofit supported by readers like you. Become a member to make stories like these free and available to everyone. Learn more at </i><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjhkill-l-th/" target="_blank" ><i>suntimes.com/member</i></a><i>.</i></p>
entries.author: Matt Moore

entries.title: Pilot of small plane killed during crash in unincorporated Deerfield
entries.summary: <p>Before the pilot of a small plane was killed, the aircraft hit the roof-area of a nearby townhome and two gas meters Wednesday night in unincorporated Deerfield. </p><p>Deputies responded to the 800 block of Swallow Street around 9:45 p.m. for a downed aircraft and found the single-engine aircraft crashed behind a row of townhomes, according to the Lake County sheriff's office. </p><p>The unidentified pilot, who was the only occupant, was pronounced dead at the scene, officials said. </p><p>The aircraft struck the roof-area of one of the townhomes as well as two gas meters which caused a "small" gas leak, authorities said. Nicor Gas officials were at the scene and the leak was controlled. </p><p>The Lake County sheriff’s office are investigating with the Federal Aviation Administration. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Chicago Symphony Orchestra’s 2026-27 season to feature Yo-Yo Ma, a lot of Beethoven and imprint of Klaus Mäkelä
entries.summary: <p>The Chicago Symphony Orchestra announced its 2026-27 lineup Thursday, featuring everyone from <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/culture-the-arts/classical/2025/08/25/yo-yo-ma-chicago-angelique-kidjo-concert" target="_blank" ><u>Yo-Yo Ma</u></a> to <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/arts-culture/2026/02/24/andrew-bird-debut-chicago-symphony-orchestra" target="_blank" ><u>Andrew Bird</u></a>. The lineup also includes a world premiere from composer Mason Bates, a season-long deep dive into Beethoven and a healthy showing of Finnish music.</p><p>That last part is thanks to CSO’s incoming music director, <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/culture-the-arts/classical/2026/02/26/klaus-makela-conductor-cso-chicago-art-institute-tour" target="_blank" ><u>Klaus Mäkelä</u></a>. Although the 30-year-old Finnish conductor and cellist will not officially be in the role until <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/2024/04/02/chicago-symphony-orchestra-names-klaus-makela-music-director" target="_blank" ><u>the following season</u></a>, Mäkelä’s is already helping shape what gets played inside Orchestra Hall.</p><p>“I have lots of music that I really want to bring to the orchestra. and this is just a couple pieces,” Mäkelä said from his backstage office this week. “I bring music that I think the orchestra should play. I also bring music that I think the audience should hear, and some things that I really just think are fun.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Klaus Makela is increasingly spending more time in Chicago. The art-loving maestro recently walked WBEZ/Chicago Sun-Times through his favorite route in the Art Institute of Chicago. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>That fun includes William Walton “Belshazzar’s Feast,” which Mäkelä will conduct in October. “It’s an incredible masterpiece. If I would buy a ticket to one concert, that would be the concert,” said Mäkelä, who is in town for the announcement and to conduct a <a class="Link" href="https://cso.org/performances/25-26/cso-featured-concerts/makela-conducts-the-rite-of-spring" target="_blank" ><u>showcase of 20th century compositions</u></a>, including Igor Stravinsky’s “The Rite of Spring.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-hangRight> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-f90000" name="image-f90000"></a>
